sentences.text: Right.

Well, the general direction was the closest possible to the near-- as close as the-- the border, it was It wasn't really important, probably, which city we're going to hit, or at least to escape to go over the border to be under the Russian regime.
At this point, I don't think it was important to our parents where they're going to take us at this point.
But as far as I could remember is we-- we went about 80 kilometers.
I don't know how to exchange that in miles, but it wasn't very far when--
About 80 kilometers through the roads, little villages, stopping here, stopping there.
And, of course, because of the airplanes that were all over us, fast, nobody could go with so much old and young kids.
And it was-- it was a very-- a terrible ordeal.
This is as far as we could make it when the-- we-- somebody came ahead of us telling us there's no use to go any further because the Germans already ahead of us.
And they are pushing us back.
They're going to come and they will push us back, going back to Kovno.
There's nowhere to escape anymore because the border-- the Russian border-- was already-- was still too far from us.
So we turned around, and we start heading back home.
Her feet were a disaster.
They were so swollen.
They were so with boils, and-- and probably the-- positively the wrong shoes-wearing and everything else.
So every time somebody else could catch and put her on something, or my father on the bicycle, kept her moving on as much as possible to help her out.
We reached Kovno again, not direct to our house because our home was in a very different direction.
So the Germans pushed us into an area where there was-- it was a school area, and a lot of empty space.
And they told us to stay there for a while.
The Germans caught-- caught on with us on the road--
Yes, and they overtook us and they told us to go, and directed us to a certain place in Kovno to get in there.
I don't know about Lithuanians.
I only-- all I knew were the Jewish people.
The Jews that lived there.
If there were some politically involved or whatever, there must have been some of them, too.
But I-- I wouldn't really know anything about it.
We were pushed into that place and separated immediately.
There were right away, I must say, Lithuanian police, where they and Germans-- that they were already like brothers and sisters and helped each other right away what to do.
What do we do now with the Jews?
So they separated the man from the women right away, on each side, and two other sides.
And-- and, of course, the women had the children and the men were separated.
And worst impact that had on me at this point was somebody was screaming.
Some woman was carrying on in a most horrible way, screaming to her husband.
And whatever the reason was, I don't know.
And her husband started-- he broke through his lines from the men and he was running towards his wife.
And a German shot him right there in front of all our eyes.
And all his guts went out of his body.
And that was the first time, really, in my life that I saw somebody shooting into a person, and-- and just the most horrible way.
Looking at it, it was-- it was shock, it was-- I could-- I'll never forget that feeling and that horrible, horrible feeling.
We were taken from there to a place called the Seventh Fort.
Just a day.
A day, and then they told us to move on to another area.
That's called the Seventh Fort.
It's a little bit outskirts of Kovno.
Yes.
Yes.
The men were, of course, taken separately, and the women and the children in a different barracks, which were underneath the, kind of, little hilly thing.
The barracks were inside of a hill.
This is the kind of place it was.
And they were made out of wood, places where you could-- you slept, like one on top of each other.
This is still summer '41.
On foot.
We walked.
Kovno is not a big city.
And we stayed there for a week.
We were hardly given any food.
There was nowhere to wash.
There was nowhere to-- any facilities, bathrooms.
They let us go only in a place where it was a big hole, and you just went there to-- whatever you had to do.
No water.

And if this wasn't enough, [INAUDIBLE] almost every night, Germans and Lithuanians used to come in and take out women, younger women, rape them, sometimes throw them back, and sometimes you never saw them again.
And that was for seven nights-- seven days and seven nights-- that we struggled there to be together and hide each other.
My mother was constantly sleeping over my oldest sister to cover her-- they shouldn't see her.
And this is how she escaped from that disaster, not to be raped there.
What happened to the men we didn't know at this point.
Until the seventh day, they told us to go home.
We could all go home because they're going to open up a ghetto, and we're going to be all put into a ghetto, so they don't need us there anymore.
So we just walked home.
But before we left this place, we had a terrible-- we smelled something horrible.
It was like a fire that is burning flesh.
You could smell-- in those days, being as stupid as I was, I said, Mommy, is there somebody burning a chicken?
Because in those days, you used to hold the chicken over a fire to take all her feathers out.
You wouldn't remember that.
You're too young.
But a chicken was not being bought in those days like you buy it now, all clean.
You used to flick it and used to do all the cleaning, and then come home and burn over a little fire to burn off all the-- that's the kind of smell it was, and even worse.
It was so, so bad, and we didn't understand what was going on.
We found out later a disastrous thing what happened, really.
We got home.
I'll come to that point.
Of course, we didn't know much about our father.
Nothing at all, as a matter of fact.
And we tried to put ourselves together.
Our house was there.
Nobody broke in.
Everything was fine.
And a couple of days later, we had somebody that came in to tell us that they saw my father in the street among prisoners on the street, going in the middle of the city.
Where did you see it?
So they told us which prison this man will be-- how my father was there.
My mother immediately ran there, and she-- sure enough, she found out that my father is there, and he's very sick.
And so she put on an unbelievable effort to bring him back home because he was very, very sick.
Actually what he had was pneumonia.
And they let him come home.
So we had finally our family is back together.
And my father told us what really happened to all the men when they were also separated from us and also taken to Seventh Fort.
They were all laying in a ditch, in a big, but very large ditch, because there were thousands of men there together.
The fort was, like, built all around.
The Germans and Lithuanians were standing all around and the men were laying outside.
They were not given little barracks like we did.
They were just laying outside.
Hundreds were taken out from there to do some work, and they never came back.
Some of them, they were left.
About 6, 7 o'clock in the evening, the Germans used to scream, 'Lay down.' So whoever wherever stood had to lay down immediately.
If you moved a hand or a leg, if you fell on somebody and it wasn't comfortable or whatever, the Germans and Lithuanians noticed that, and they used to fire direct into that person that moved.
That means if he was injured or he was dead, he was laying there among the live ones for seven consecutive days.

At the end, when they decided that they are going to clean this place out, the women and the men, what they did to the men was every dead person, or not completely dead-- four men had to take that person, go up on the hill, throw him behind the other side of the hill, and these four would stand in front of the German or Lithuanian, whoever was there.
They would shoot into these four men, and they themselves would fall in over to the other side.
And this is how they got rid of all the men that were there-- I don't know how many hundreds, or couple of thousand.
When my father's turn came to do the same thing, he was already one of the last, last ones.
There were maybe 30 or 37 people, if I remember he told me.
He told us that he had to take, he and another three men, a body and go up the hill.
What I meant is go up the hill-- on the other side was also the same kind of ditch.
Like, they were here-- on the other side of the hill was also like that.
So they just threw all the dead ones there.
They picked up that man, that dead man, and they went up the hill, threw them into the other side.
And while they had to face now the German or the Lithuanian, one of the Lithuanians looked at my father and he said to him, take off your boots.
I like them.
I would like to have them.
So he sat down and started to take the boots off.
By the way, he says, you wear a very good pair-- special pants that they went into the boots like riding pants.
This is how he was dressed to escape, if you remember what I told you, yeah?
So he said to him, all right, before I'm taking out-- giving you back my pants, too, let me just tell you what I have in my pocket that you should know.
That you are going to kill now a man that was fighting for the independence of [INAUDIBLE] Lithuania, which my father was.
He was a soldier.
At that time, it was-- at this time, it was probably 20 years that he was already as a soldier.
And he had the medal and all his papers that he was actually a volunteer in the army to fight for their independence.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He took it out from his pocket, and suddenly, he hears another voice-- hold it!
Hold it!
I want to see who is talking to me.
And there was another Lithuanian coming over.
I want to see his papers.
He comes over and he looks at his papers and he says, do you know that this man has more higher rank than I have?
And he says to my father pick up your pants, pick up your boots, and let's go.
So he picks up his boots, and he was-- when he was walking in the grass, the grass was full of blood from the people that they were killed the night before and through all those seven days.
He showed us when he came his socks were still full of blood.
Hard blood on those socks.
So my father says, where are you taking me?
So he says, I'm going to put you in another jail.
I want you-- I want you to-- I want to take you away from all this.
So he said-- my father said, no, not only me, but whoever you see here left are the same people as I am.
And if you're not helping them the way you're going to help me, then I don't want to be left-- be left alive either.
So he took this whole last group, about 37 people, and he led them-- he put them into a little shack there.
And that smell that we smelled-- that was that they threw-- they poured over these bodies something of burning, and they burned all these bodies.
And when we left the day-- the same day we smelled-- that was the smell.
And all the other people were burned.
Now, my father, and together with these couple of people, were taken-- this is when somebody saw them going, a friend of my family or whoever.
I doubt-- I cannot remember who it was.
And this is when they brought him into that jail in Kovno.
Now they're in Kovno, and my father is now with us, and trying to recuperate from his pneumonia that he had.
And--
It was still summer, and this is-- this is all I'm telling you is days--
It's weeks-- not even a couple of weeks.
And then we had an order from the Germans that everybody should start packing.
All the Jews have to pack because they're going to give us a certain area in Kovno where they're going to open up-- open up a ghetto.
The army.
The army, yes.
Radio.
And plaques on the-- on poles in the street that was written to us.
And newspapers.
That the Jews-- all the Jews, wherever they live, have to take their belongings and move on to a place called Slabodka.
Spelling-- S-L-A-B-O-T-K-A.
Kovno had a river, and separated in two parts.
We lived on one side of [INAUDIBLE] Slabodka was on the other side of the river.
Yes, it's kind of a neighborhood.
Yes.
Yes, exactly.
And it was a little, I would call, lower class of an area.
Yes, a lot.
There was a very famous Slabodka yeshiva, they called it-- most famous in the whole world.
And very, very prominent rabbis came out from that area.
So Slabodka is not just-- it was not a very outgoing place, and not too many rich people lived there, but the yeshiva was one of the best in the world, and incredible.
We had to find a place, so most of the Lithuanians that lived there took over the Jewish homes in the other side of the river, and this is how we exchanged homes with the Lithuanians.

Most of the Lithuanians that lived there were very, very poor, and now they moved into lovely builded homes that the Jewish people-- they were very-- not very, very rich, but very good-- in good shape.
Jewish people had very-- they were, all of them, businesspeople and teachers and music people.
And you-- it was a very-- Lithuanian Jews were known as very learned people, and I would say elite of the Jewish European people.
It was a very modern and outgoing and sophisticated country, Lithuania.
As bad as it was, the Lithuanians, but the Jews were very sophisticated, and living that way, too.
We didn't have many Hasidim there, but religious people of sophistication was in them.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was 19-- that was 1941.
We all moved in in, I think it was in-- at this point, August that we were all put into the ghetto.
It was closed up with wires all around us.
And there were Lithuanians and German-- Lithuanian police and German army, always watching us.
We couldn't have big and wide apartments or homes, so they pushed us in, sometimes two or three families in one house.
My parents found a tiny, little house for the six of us.
And right away, the first week came-- they call it [INAUDIBLE]-- action.
But we call it [INAUDIBLE].
Immediately they came, the Germans, with two big trucks, and we had to give away a lot of our belongings.
Anything that had any value.
So every day was another story.
One day they came and took away from us all our electrical appliances.
Another time they came and took away anything that had a value silver, gold, money, watches-- anything that had value.
All the jewelry-- you had to give it away.
Then they started to clear out the ghetto with people.
Yes, from house to house.
They told us to come to a certain place and leave it there.
But besides that, they came from house to house to search.
Like I said, everything we had that we managed to bring in from our house in Kovno.
Was my mother's-- all her jewelery that she had, silver-- everything that was made of silver.
I don't have to tell you-- flatware.
And, as matter of fact, I remember so clearly she had-- we had a beautiful silver electric water-- like to make-- to make tea.
Electric.
It was pure silver.
It was taken away.
Irons-- even that.
And all the money.
I don't know.
I really-- that, I can't tell.
I don't know how much my mother or father, my parents-- had money.
What they did with it.
And I'm sure that was given away.
And if they had something to hide, I don't know if they would-- they could even hide it, because everything was touched.
And the clothing-- everything was watched so carefully by them if it was hidden somewhere.
So actually, what we were left with is just personal belongings and bedding.
Pots and pans to cook if you could have food.
That was another problem.
How do you get food?
They, most of the time, took-- to break our spirits down, the first thing what they did is they took most of intelligentsia out of the-- of the ghetto, and we never seen them again.
They used to tell everybody that they're taking them somewhere to work.
And-- but they never came back.
So at this point, we never knew really what happened to them.
But we knew that they are-- they were killed.
Knowing deeply in us, we all knew that they were killed.
Parents, yes.
They knew that this is-- this is the way they do it.
And so by 500 people in one time, 1,000 people another time.
Every couple of weeks something was happening, and it became shorter-- smaller and smaller, the-- the-- the population.
Then they told-- where we lived, they asked us to move in-- deeper into the middle of the area because not too many people were left already, so they kept on making smaller the ghetto.
We had to move again.
So we moved again to another house, and this is when we found ourselves to live together with another family.
And we shared the kitchen together.
It was a mother and her daughter.
I can't remember their names.
But we became like sisters, and my mother and the other lady became like two sisters, and the daughter was like another sister to us.
Meanwhile, we were still together.
And it was a very unusual thing because they didn't touch any of us yet.
So we were the four daughters, my father, my mother together-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until that big, disastrous day, October the 28th, 1942.
Was it still in '41 or '42?
I think it was '41.
October-- yeah, that must be in '41 because that was already towards the end of the year.
They asked the whole ghetto people get out of their homes and come to a very, very big place.
It was an empty place.
A square.
We all of us had to come out there.
There were many Germans and Lithuanians.
And they start making groups.
Every family stood together and lined up like that.
You kept on coming closer to the German's line, and they told you, you go to the left, and this goes to the right.
And this is how they segregated us.
We didn't know why.
We didn't know what the reason was.
But they just moved left, right, left, right.
Sometimes the whole family, sometimes individuals.
When it came to our line, my grandmother was sent to one side, but our family was sent to the-- all of us together-- to the other side.
So we are-- now we're still together, but my grandmother was the first one to go.
When they did that, certain-- that area, which was the left side, they told us all to go back to our homes.
And the other side of people-- we saw that they were taken away out of the ghetto.
And when we noticed where they are going, we know-- we knew they're taking them to a place called the Ninth Fort.
The Ninth Fort was nearer to the ghetto area.
The Seventh Fort what I mentioned before was in a different direction.
This was the Ninth Fort.
And, of course, we didn't know why they were taken there, but late at night, we heard a lot, a lot of shooting.
And there were 9,200 people shot in one night and burned to death.
That broke down the ghetto in the most horrible way.
I mean, that was like every family was hit already at this point.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the pain was hard to describe in everybody's home.
